did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning techniques for skin cancer classification. Specifically, it employed Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) for feature extraction from skin lesion images and utilized the One-Step Secant (OSS) algorithm for model training to classify melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a study comparing neural network performance using GLCM features on a skin cancer dataset. It includes specific sections for Methodology (dataset description, pre-processing, feature extraction, OSS training function) and Results and Discussions (confusion matrices, ROC curves, accuracy metrics for three different methods). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICE3IS), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comparison of Neural Network Performance Based on Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix Features
journal: 2024 4th International Conference on Electronic and Electrical Engineering and Intelligent System (ICE3IS)
brief_summary: This study compared three neural network methods for skin cancer classification using images of melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis from the Kaggle dataset. The researchers applied Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) feature extraction combined with adaptive histogram equalization and entropy enhancement. Model training utilized the One-Step Secant (OSS) algorithm, with performance evaluated via confusion matrices, ROC curves, and accuracy metrics across different hidden layer sizes and feature sets. Method 3, which used 7 offsets and 4 extraction features, achieved the highest average validation accuracy of 73% and demonstrated the best consistency. However, the authors concluded that the overall accuracy of approximately 73% was insufficient for reliable real-world clinical diagnosis, necessitating more robust future methods.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three classes in the Kaggle dataset used for skin cancer classification. Initial count: 521 images; refined count: 299 images.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer diagnostic categories (melanoma, nevus, seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions into three discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis. The study compares neural network performance using GLCM features and the One-Step Secant (OSS) algorithm, evaluating accuracy, confusion matrices, and ROC curves for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection
datasets_used.description: A skin cancer image dataset sourced from Kaggle, originally containing images of melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis. The authors refined the dataset to address class imbalances by curating and removing images to ensure balanced representation across the three classes.

use.training: Used for training neural networks using the One-Step Secant (OSS) algorithm with cross-validation.
use.validation: Used for validation to optimize performance and tune model parameters.
use.testing: Used for testing to evaluate model performance via confusion matrices, ROC curves, and accuracy metrics.
use.other: The dataset was pre-processed by converting images to grayscale, applying Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE), and resizing. It was also balanced to mitigate bias towards the majority class (nevus).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the dataset into ten folds, using nine for training and one for testing in each iteration, to provide a more accurate and reliable estimate of model performance.
To describe the performance of the classification model by mapping predicted versus actual values and assessing accuracy based on the main diagonal value.
To evaluate the model's ability to distinguish between positive and negative classes and compare performance across different methods and hidden layer sizes.
To assess the model's performance in the training process, specifically looking at the number of epochs required to achieve convergence to identify efficiency or overfitting.
To report the variability and stability of accuracy metrics (training, validation, and testing) across different folds and hidden layer configurations.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA, p-values) was reported in the paper. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (mean accuracy, standard deviation) and visual inspection of ROC curves.

Method 1.description: 4 offsets and 4 extraction features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity)
Method 1.notes: ROC curve shape close to inverted L at 10 hidden layers indicating good performance.

Method 2.description: 4 offsets and 7 extraction features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity, entropy matrix, entropy result, IDM)
Method 2.notes: ROC curve favorable at 20 hidden layers but performance slightly lower than Method 1.

Method 3.description: 7 offsets and 4 extraction features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity)
Method 3.notes: Consistently exhibited good ROC performance across all hidden layers, proving superior to Methods 1 and 2. Best consistency and stability.

Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma De.overall_conclusion: Method 3 achieved the highest average validation accuracy of 73% and demonstrated the best consistency. However, the overall accuracy of approximately 73% was deemed insufficient for reliable real-world clinical diagnosis.

architecture_used.main_model: Artificial Neural Network (ANN) trained with the One-Step Secant (OSS) algorithm (trainoss)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) and Entropy Enhancement

method_1.description: 4 offsets and 4 extraction features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity)

method_2.description: 4 offsets and 7 extraction features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity, entropy matrix, entropy result, IDM)

method_3.description: 7 offsets and 4 extraction features (contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity)

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly named; error calculated between predicted and expected outputs

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale conversion (converting RGB images to grayscale to remove color information while retaining structural information)
Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) using the adapthisteq function to increase local contrast and enhance texture details
Entropy enhancement to clarify structure and texture by optimizing pixel intensity distribution
Dataset balancing/curation (refining the dataset by removing images to address class imbalances between melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis)
Resizing (images were resized as part of the pre-processing pipeline)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The authors addressed class imbalances by meticulously curating and removing images from the initial Kaggle dataset to ensure balanced representation across the three classes (melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis). The refined dataset consisted of 299 melanoma, 1261 nevus, and 226 seborrheic keratosis images.

conclusions: Method 3, which utilized 7 offsets and 4 extraction features (contrast, correlation, energy, and homogeneity), demonstrated the best performance in terms of consistency and stability across all hidden layers compared to Methods 1 and 2.
Method 3 achieved the highest average validation accuracy of 73% with a standard deviation of 0%, indicating superior stability.
ROC analysis confirmed that Method 3 consistently exhibited good performance across all hidden layer sizes, proving superior to Methods 1 and 2.
The overall accuracy of approximately 73% achieved by the models is insufficient for reliable real-world clinical diagnosis of skin cancer.
The study concludes that the methods explored do not meet the necessary standards for practical medical diagnostics due to the stringent accuracy requirements.
Future research must focus on developing more advanced techniques to achieve the high accuracy and reliability required for effective skin cancer detection.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges that the achieved accuracy of approximately 73% is insufficient for reliable real-world clinical diagnosis, concluding that the methods do not meet the stringent accuracy requirements for practical medical diagnostics.

The dataset was manually curated and balanced by removing images to address class imbalance, resulting in a refined dataset of 1,786 images (299 melanoma, 1,261 nevus, 226 seborrheic keratosis) from the original Kaggle dataset, which may introduce selection bias.
The study relies on a single dataset sourced from Kaggle without external validation, limiting the generalizability of the findings to other populations or imaging devices.

The research employs traditional machine learning (ANN with GLCM features) rather than deep learning, which may limit performance compared to state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks in dermatology.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (mean accuracy, standard deviation) and visual inspection of ROC curves.

The One-Step Secant (OSS) algorithm was used for training, which is less common in modern deep learning contexts compared to backpropagation-based optimizers like Adam or SGD, potentially affecting convergence and performance.
